explicit_text: I was born in Chicago in 1931 and lived on the Northwest side around Laramie and Fullerton when I enlisted in the military in 1950. Before joining the service, I worked for the Chicago Park District's Rainbow Fleet during the summers, teaching people how to sail and running the crash boat. Chicago was really home for me during those early years, and it shaped a lot of my experiences before I went off to serve.

explicit_text: I was attending DePaul University on a swim scholarship when the Korean War started in June 1950. I was balancing school and swimming until I enlisted in the Marine Corps on December 31, 1950, which meant I had to quit school shortly after. explicit_text: I enlisted in the United States Marine Corps on December 31, 1950, after deciding against the Coast Guard due to their long waiting list. My training started at Glenview Naval Air Station, where I went through boot camp and various duties before being sent to El Toro Marine Corps Air Station. I served as a rifleman and later worked in flight safety equipment, eventually deploying overseas during the Korean War era.

explicit_text: My Marine Corps boot camp was held at Glenview Naval Air Station, where about 60 of us trained in two platoons. It was there that I first experienced military discipline and was introduced to the Marine Corps squadron. The base also had a swim team I briefly joined before shipping out, and it was a key starting point for my military career.

explicit_text: After boot camp, I was transferred to El Toro Marine Corps Air Station in California, where I did mess duty and worked in the motor pool driving dump trucks and semi-trucks. I also helped as a carpenter's helper and eventually joined the swim team there temporarily. El Toro was where I transitioned from basic duties to more specialized roles before heading overseas.

explicit_text: After El Toro, our squadron was transferred by train to San Diego, where we were billeted alphabetically. I met two other Smiths there and spent time at Humphrey's Tavern, a popular off-base spot. San Diego was a staging area before we boarded the USS Sitkoh Bay and headed overseas.

explicit_text: We landed in Osaka, Japan, during our deployment, and I had liberty there. On my second day of liberty, I was tasked with picking up some shirts from a Japanese cleaner for a former drill instructor. I had a close call with curfew and left the shirts in a rickshaw, which led to a report and potential disciplinary action, but it was eventually dismissed.
